weeklyStories.headline: The government wants first dibs on AI models. Here's what that means for you: nothing yet.
weeklyStories.body: The White House signed an executive order asking AI companies to let federal agencies test new models for 30 days before public release. It's voluntary — no one's being blocked, and no tools you use are changing tomorrow.
weeklyStories.whyYouCare: If your company uses ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini, future model updates might come with a short delay while the government kicks the tires. Right now? Zero impact on your Monday. But it signals regulators are watching the 'how powerful is too powerful' question more closely — which eventually shapes what features you get and when.
weeklyStories.cocktailParty: The government wants a 30-day preview of new AI models before release. It's voluntary and changes nothing today, but tells you where regulation is headed.
weeklyStories.preview: The White House signed an executive order asking AI companies to let federal agencies test new models for 30 days bef...

weeklyStories.headline: Anthropic's AI is writing code for other AI. Here's what that actually means.
weeklyStories.body: Anthropic announced that their Claude models are now generating code used to build and improve other AI systems. This is NOT the AI 'training itself' (that's a different, scarier thing) — it's more like a senior developer writing the scaffolding that a junior developer will work inside.
weeklyStories.whyYouCare: This is why AI tools keep getting better so fast. The companies building them are using AI to speed up their own development cycle. For you: expect more frequent updates, new features landing faster, and the gap between 'cutting edge' and 'what's in your work tools' getting shorter. The pace isn't slowing down.
weeklyStories.cocktailParty: Anthropic is using AI to write code that builds other AI — not self-aware sci-fi stuff, more like using a power tool to build better power tools. It's why your AI apps update every two weeks now.
weeklyStories.preview: Anthropic announced that their Claude models are now generating code used to build and improve other AI systems.

weeklyStories.headline: OpenAI built an entire product without any human writing code. The humans did something harder.
weeklyStories.body: An OpenAI team shipped a complete product where AI coding agents wrote all the code. But the humans weren't sitting on a beach — they spent their time designing the system the AI worked inside: setting constraints, writing specs, building guardrails, defining what 'good' looks like. They're calling it 'harness engineering.'
weeklyStories.whyYouCare: This is the Episode 2 lesson playing out at the highest level. The skill isn't doing the work yourself — it's knowing how to describe what you want clearly enough that the AI produces something usable. The better your brief, the better the output. This is prompting, scaled up to building entire products. Same muscle, bigger stage.
weeklyStories.cocktailParty: OpenAI built a product where AI wrote all the code. The humans designed the environment it worked in — constraints, specs, quality bars. The new skill isn't coding. It's knowing exactly what to ask for.

siteLinks.label: Episode 2: Tell Me What You Want

weeklyStories.preview: An OpenAI team shipped a complete product where AI coding agents wrote all the code.

weeklyStories.headline: An AI system processed 7,000 tax returns at 97% accuracy. The accountants made it better, not redundant.
weeklyStories.body: OpenAI and Thrive built a system where AI drafts tax returns, human accountants review and correct them, and the system improves from those corrections in real time. It processed 7,000 returns and saved about a third of prep time per return.
weeklyStories.whyYouCare: This is the future of professional work in one story: AI does the first draft, you make it right, and the system gets better because of your expertise — not despite it. Your professional judgment doesn't get replaced. It gets amplified. The people who know their domain best become the ones whose corrections train the system. If that sounds like your job with extra steps, you're paying attention.
weeklyStories.cocktailParty: AI did 7,000 tax returns at 97% accuracy. But the humans reviewing it made it better over time. The model doesn't replace expertise — it gets trained BY expertise. That's the pattern for every professional field.

dailyHeadlines.headline: Google made Gemini 2.5 the default in every product you already use.
dailyHeadlines.body: Search, Docs, Gmail, Workspace — all quietly switched to Google's latest AI model this week. You didn't opt in. You didn't get asked. It just got better in the background.
dailyHeadlines.whyYouCare: If you use Google at work, you're already using a more capable AI than you were last week — whether you noticed or not. The AI features in your Docs sidebar, your Gmail summaries, your search results: all upgraded. This is how AI adoption actually happens for most people. Not by choosing a tool. By the tools you already use choosing for you.
dailyHeadlines.cocktailParty: Google switched its AI to a new model across everything — Gmail, Docs, Search. You're already using it. You just didn't get a memo.

siteLinks.label: Episode 1: On Wednesdays We Use AI

dailyHeadlines.preview: Search, Docs, Gmail, Workspace — all quietly switched to Google's latest AI model this week.

dailyHeadlines.headline: Apple Intelligence is finally fully live. Yes, the thing they promised a year ago.
dailyHeadlines.body: All the AI features Apple announced last year — smart Siri, writing tools, notification summaries, image generation — are now actually available in the iOS 19 beta. After a staggered rollout that tested everyone's patience.
dailyHeadlines.whyYouCare: If you have an iPhone, you're about to get the AI assistant Apple promised but couldn't deliver until now. Writing help in any text field. Smarter Siri that can actually do multi-step tasks. Notification summaries that work. This isn't a new app to download — it's baked into the phone you already have. Check Settings → Apple Intelligence.
dailyHeadlines.cocktailParty: Apple Intelligence is finally fully live after a year of delays. If you have a recent iPhone, you now have an AI assistant built in — no app needed, just turn it on in Settings.
dailyHeadlines.preview: All the AI features Apple announced last year — smart Siri, writing tools, notification summaries, image generation —...

dailyHeadlines.headline: Microsoft Copilot Pro costs $30/month per person. Companies are doing the math.
dailyHeadlines.body: Enterprise AI tools are hitting the budget conversation no one prepared for. Microsoft's Copilot Pro is $30/seat/month — for a 500-person company, that's $180,000 a year. One CFO called it 'the new Salesforce creep.'
dailyHeadlines.whyYouCare: If your company hasn't given you AI tools yet, this might be why. The per-person cost is making leadership ask hard questions: Who actually needs it? How do we measure ROI? Are people even using it? If you want your company to invest in AI tools for your team, you need to be ready to show what you'd do with them. Episode 2 is your cheat sheet for that conversation.
dailyHeadlines.cocktailParty: Microsoft is charging $30/month per person for AI at work. For big companies, that's hundreds of thousands a year. It's why your IT department hasn't turned it on yet — and why being able to articulate what you'd DO with it matters.

siteLinks.label: Episode 2: Tell Me What You Want

dailyHeadlines.preview: Enterprise AI tools are hitting the budget conversation no one prepared for.
